title: The Nicene Creed, Part 7
speaker: Elder Darin Hughey
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 7 (Elder Darin Hughey).txt
outline: Part 7 of the Nicene Creed series
Exposition of the relevant article(s) of the Nicene Creed
summary: Part 7 of the October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, presented by Elder Darin Hughey. Continues the verse-by-verse exposition of the Creed in its historical and theological context.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading and Introduction

Often when there is a lecture series or teaching series, you bring your heavy hitter, your most eloquent speaker, to be the keynote speaker — to bring it all together and put a bow on it. I am not a keynote speaker. I am not an eloquent speaker. But I take comfort in what God said to Paul in 2 Corinthians: "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness."

Over the past few weeks we have looked at — not an exhaustive study of the Nicene Creed — but a close look at a few key truths laid out in the Creed. For the first lesson, Pastor Tito Lyro gave us an introduction: the historical background behind the Creed, why the council was assembled, who the men were who wrote it, and why they wrote it. Next, Elder Nick Anderson taught on "We believe" — the importance of truth and God's word, and why we should fight for it and never stray from it. Elder Scott Hollander taught on "only begotten" and "one substance" — we learned about God as he reveals himself in his word, the deity of Christ, and the doctrine of the Trinity. Mr. Isaiah Couch taught on "for us and for our salvation" — the incarnation and the work of Christ on our behalf. Mr. Alex Lyro taught on "who proceeds from the Father and the Son" — the work of the Holy Spirit and his role in the Trinity. And this morning, Mr. Steve Thomas taught on "one holy Catholic and apostolic church" — a topic that is much needed in the church today, teaching on the importance of Christ's bride, the church.

And that brings us to today's section: "We acknowledge one baptism for the remission of sins."

My goals for the afternoon are three things: to look at what is meant by "one baptism," what is meant by "for the remission of sins," and then to work through six summary statements from Kevin DeYoung's book at the end.

I read the entire Creed again this afternoon because I wanted to point out a change that the council made in this section, compared to the previous sections. Did you notice a change in the language? Up until this section, the Creed has used the language "we believe" — *we believe in one God, we believe in one Lord Jesus Christ, we believe in the Holy Spirit, we believe in one holy Catholic and apostolic church*. But now there is a change. The Nicene Council now writes: **"We acknowledge one baptism for the remission of sins."** Why the change from "we believe" to "we acknowledge"?

## Why "We Acknowledge" Instead of "We Believe"

**First**, the Creed says "we believe" in relation to God, but "we acknowledge" in relation to baptism, because we place our belief, faith, and trust in the one true God of the Bible. They chose "we believe" for God because it shows that our confidence is in our covenant-keeping God — not in the sign of baptism itself. We believe in and trust God because he saves us by his grace; but we *acknowledge* our baptism and what it represents. Our baptism does not save us, but it is the outward identification of what we profess — namely, our union with Christ.

**Second**, all the "we believe" statements in the Creed are matters of a self-sufficient, essential nature. As we have learned these past few Sundays, failure to believe what the Nicene Creed teaches puts us outside of Christian orthodoxy.

> *"Therefore I said to you that you will die in your sins; for if you do not believe that I am he, you will die in your sins."* (John 8:24)

> *"Jesus said to them, 'Most assuredly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I AM.' Then they took up stones to throw at him."* (John 8:58)

In these texts, Jesus claims to be God. The deity of Christ is at the heart of Christian doctrine. These things are to be *believed* — not merely acknowledged.

**Third**, while baptism is important — we are commanded to be baptized in the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) — baptism does not save us. We *believe* the truth claims about God; we *acknowledge* our baptism.

## One Baptism: What Does "One" Mean?

**First**, "one baptism" may indicate that baptism is a once-for-all act. We should only be baptized once. Baptism is not for getting right with God after a season of sin — that is what confession and repentance are for. Baptism is the *initiatory* sign of new life in Christ, showing that you are part of his covenant family. The Lord's Supper is the *renewing* sign, observed as often as we celebrate it together. If a person has a valid baptism — and to be valid, a baptism needs to be Trinitarian, administered with water by a recognized ordained minister of a Christian church — that person should not be baptized again.

**Second**, we acknowledge one baptism because there are not different baptisms for different kinds of Christians. As we learned this morning, Christ has one bride and one church. We read from Ephesians 4 this afternoon. Remember that in Ephesians 2 and 3, Paul is dealing with Jewish-Gentile conflicts — the Gentiles are now fellow heirs and recipients of the gospel, and the dividing wall has been cast down. Then he declares in Ephesians 4:4–6:

> *"There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called in one hope of your calling; one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all, who is above all, and through all, and in you all."* (Ephesians 4:4–6)

There is one baptism — not a separate baptism for Jews and another for Gentiles. Think about what Paul says in 1 Corinthians, where some were claiming to be baptized into Peter, or Stephanas, or Paul — and he emphatically says: no. There is not a baptism according to your favorite theologian. There is not one baptism for men and another for women. There is not one baptism for Africans and one for Europeans. There is not one for rich and another for poor, or for slave and free. There is only one baptism, because there is only one Father, one Lord, one Spirit, one church, and one faith.

As Kevin DeYoung says in his book: "It is a profound element of our unity to think that people who are very different from you — who grew up in different parts of the country or the world, who have different kinds of sins, temptations, and struggles, different kinds of jobs, who live in different places — and yet we are all named in baptism: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit."

## "For the Remission of Sins": What Does This Mean?

The second, perhaps more difficult question: what is meant by "baptism for the remission of sins"? The first thing that comes to mind for many is baptismal regeneration. There is no doubt that the New Testament connects baptism and forgiveness. In baptism, the water symbolizes cleansing and purification. It is tied to the forgiveness of sins in Acts 2, the washing away of sins in Acts 22, and in Titus 3 baptism is called "the washing of regeneration and renewal." So baptism is a sign and seal of forgiveness. There is no question that baptism and faith should be linked, and that baptism and forgiveness should be linked. But *how*?

Some would read "for the remission of sins" in a quid pro quo relationship — get baptized, receive forgiveness. I do not think that is what the Nicene Council meant to say.

The Nicene Creed was originally written in Greek. When I get into Greek and parts of speech, I rely heavily on Kevin DeYoung's book and his explanation. The word translated here as "for" is the Greek preposition *eis*, which can mean "for," "in," or "into." It is the common preposition used with baptism in the scriptural texts.

> *"Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them **in** [eis] the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit."* (Matthew 28:19)

> *"Therefore we were buried with him through baptism **into** [eis] death, that just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life."* (Romans 6:4)

All these texts use the preposition *eis*. To baptize *eis* — into — is to be baptized in the name, in the Triune name of God, or to be baptized into Christ's death: both express identity as belonging to God. That preposition speaks of identification. When you are baptized in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, it identifies you as belonging to God. The physical sign of baptism points to these spiritual realities.

In the same way, to be baptized "for" or "into" the remission of sins does not mean that the act of pouring water automatically and of itself forgives or makes clean. Rather, it identifies — it points to — that forgiveness, and it identifies us as God's beloved people.

## Six Summary Statements from Kevin DeYoung

At the end of his book on the Nicene Creed, Kevin DeYoung offers six summary statements. Let me work through each of them.

**1. The Creed reminds us of the importance of faith.** If our confession falls outside of all the "we believe" statements found in the Creed, we are not part of the small-*c* catholic or orthodox church. This means that "Christian" in a historic and orthodox sense does *not* include Mormons or Unitarians, who by definition deny the Trinity. It also does not include theological liberals who would not affirm the virgin birth, or that Christ rose bodily from the dead, or that he is coming again to judge the living and the dead. If those facts are denied, one stands outside the bounds of historic orthodox Christianity.

**2. The history of the Nicene Creed shows that new elements are sometimes necessary to combat new errors.** One way to say this: the Nicene Creed is a floor, not a ceiling. The Creed is what Christians *must* believe, but it is not everything Christians *should* believe. Some want to make it a ceiling, but the history of the Creed itself tells us that is not what the Nicene Council intended. In 325, the council wrote the original Creed — but only one generation later, by 381, another council met. The theology was the same, but the statement was expanded because of new theological errors. After that, in 431 there was a council at Ephesus, and in 451 another council at Chalcedon — the church repeatedly dealing with Christology and Trinitarian theology and making new statements because new errors kept arising. New statements are sometimes necessary.

**3. The Nicene Creed reminds us of the centrality of the Trinity.** Most of this ancient Creed is taken up with defining, celebrating, and worshiping the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Sadly, the doctrine of the Trinity is an afterthought for many Christians. We sort of know it — usually by way of bad analogies — but since it is hard to wrap our minds around and we don't see why it is relevant to our daily Christian walk, we don't put much thought into it.

**4. We see in the Nicene Creed what we might call the significance of religion.** "Religion" is often used as a bad word. People say, "We don't have religion, we have grace," or "Jesus came to destroy religion." Well, if religion means something man-made, then of course he did. But often what people mean by "I'm spiritual, not religious" is that religion involves a list of must-dos: services to attend, clergy to be accountable to, a sacred book to read, doctrines to believe. And here we see an affirmation of religion in exactly that sense. There is a doctrine of the church; at least one of the two sacraments — baptism — is mentioned in the Creed. We cannot just say "me and Jesus, we're good" — we have a church, an order, rites, and rituals.

**5. There is a soteriological focus in the Creed.** The doctrine of the Trinity points us toward the doctrine of salvation. In the middle of the Creed, talking about the incarnation, it says "who for us and for our salvation." At the end, it speaks of "one baptism for the remission of sins." This focus on sin, salvation, judgment, and forgiveness is right and proper, because the chief problem in your life and mine is that we are sinners — and the only solution is to know God the Father, who sent his Son to die on the cross and rise again, and sent his Spirit, that we might be his people.

**6. There is a future orientation in the Creed — a looking ahead.** The very last line reads: "and we look for the resurrection of the dead." The Nicene Creed ends on a note of expectation. We look forward to the resurrection of our bodies and eternal life in the world to come. Not just life here — not just life in heaven without pain, though that will be wonderful. Can you imagine life without sin? Life with fellow believers who went before us? Life reunited with family members in the faith? All of that is a blessing we look forward to. We also look forward to life with this God that we have confessed and studied in the Nicene Creed. For eternity we will grow to know and to worship God the Father Almighty. We will bow before his only begotten Son, Jesus Christ, forever.

## Conclusion

That concludes our look at the Nicene Creed. This study has been encouraging to me. When you think that it has been around for 1,700 years and hasn't changed much, it just points to the fact that we have a faithful God who is unchanging — and for that we can be thankful.
